sections.heading: This Directive establishes minimum rules concerning the definition of criminal offences and sanctions in the area of
sections.title: Member States must implement GDPR-compliant laws by deadline
sections.summary: Member States are required to enact national laws, regulations, and administrative provisions that comply with the GDPR by 3 December 2020. They must immediately notify the European Commission of these measures and ensure their official publications include a reference to this Directive. Member States must also establish methods for making such references.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a deadline for Member States to implement GDPR-compliant laws, which affects the timeline for data transfer impact assessments in national jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Member States must enact national laws, regulations, and administrative provisions compliant with this Directive by 3 December 2020
Member States must immediately inform the Commission of these measures
Member States must ensure official publications include a reference to this Directive
Member States must establish methods for making such references

sections.source_quote: Member States shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive by 3 December 2020. They shall immediately inform the Commission thereof.
sections.relevance_reason: Core implementation deadline for GDPR compliance

sections.heading: This Directive does not apply to money laundering as regards property derived from criminal offences affecting the
sections.title: Member States report national law provisions to Commission
sections.summary: Member States must communicate the text of their main national law provisions adopted in the field covered by this Directive to the European Commission. This section also outlines reporting timelines for the Commission to assess Member States' compliance with the Directive by 2022 and 2023, including potential legislative amendments based on Member States' information.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires Member States to report national law provisions to the Commission, which impacts the transparency and accountability of data transfer mechanisms across borders.

requirements.text: Member States must communicate the text of their main national law provisions to the Commission
Commission shall submit a report to the European Parliament and Council by 3 December 2022 assessing Member States' compliance with this Directive
Commission shall submit a report to the European Parliament and Council by 3 December 2023 assessing the added value of this Directive for combating money laundering and impact on fundamental rights
Commission may present a legislative proposal to amend this Directive based on the reports

sections.heading: Member States shall take the necessary measures to ensure that:
sections.title: Cooperation in multi-member state jurisdiction
sections.summary: When multiple Member States have jurisdiction over the same offence, they must cooperate to decide which state will prosecute the offender, considering factors like offence territory, offender nationality/residency, victim country, and offender location. The matter may be referred to Eurojust for centralized proceedings.

requirements.text: Member States concerned shall cooperate to decide which will prosecute the offender when multiple Member States have jurisdiction
Account shall be taken of offence territory, offender nationality/residency, victim country, and offender location
The matter shall be referred to Eurojust where appropriate and in accordance with Article 12 of Framework Decision 2009/948/JHA

sections.source_quote: Where an offence referred to in Articles 3 and 4 falls within the jurisdiction of more than one Member State and where any of the Member States concerned can validly prosecute on the basis of the same facts, the Member States concerned shall cooperate in order to decide which of them will prosecute the offender, with the aim of centralising proceedings in a single Member State.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: multi-member state jurisdiction cooperation

sections.heading: In the case of point (c) of paragraph 3 of this Article, Member States may further require that the relevant conduct
sections.title: Additional aggravating circumstances for financial crimes
sections.summary: Member States may establish aggravating circumstances for financial crimes, including the value of laundered property or its origin from specific offences. These circumstances can lead to enhanced penalties for money laundering activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows Member States to define additional aggravating factors for financial crimes, which may influence DTIA assessments for cross-border money laundering risks.

requirements.text: Member States may provide that the following circumstances are regarded as aggravating: (a) laundered property of considerable value; (b) laundered property derives from specific offences

sections.source_quote: Member States may provide that, in relation to the offences referred to in Article 3(1) and (5) and Article 4, the following circumstances are to be regarded as aggravating circumstances: (a) the laundered property is of considerable value; or (b) the laundered property derives from one of the offences referred to in points (a) to (e) and (h) of point (1) of Article 2.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial crime amendment, no data protection link

sections.heading: Member States shall take the necessary measures to ensure that the conduct referred to in points (a) and (b) of
sections.title: Criminal penalties for specific financial offences
sections.summary: Member States must establish criminal penalties that are effective, proportionate, and dissuasive for offences defined in Articles 3 and 4. These offences relate to financial crimes such as money laundering and related activities. The penalties must apply to natural persons who commit these offences.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes minimum criminal penalties for financial crimes, which may impact DTIA requirements for risk assessment of cross-border financial crime activities.

requirements.text: Member States shall take necessary measures to ensure offences referred to in Articles 3 and 4 are punishable by effective, proportionate and dissuasive criminal penalties

sections.source_quote: Member States shall take the necessary measures to ensure that the offences referred to in Articles 3 and 4 are punishable by effective, proportionate and dissuasive criminal penalties.
sections.relevance_reason: Criminal penalties for financial crimes not directly relevant to data compliance
